payload.text: PR: refactor the auth middleware; touches session handling and token refresh logic.
payload.topic: PR: refactor the auth middleware; touches session handling and token refresh logic.

payload.subject: PR: refactor the auth middleware; touches session handling and token refresh logic.
payload.body: PR: refactor the auth middleware; touches session handling and token refresh logic.

findings: No actual diff or code provided — review is based solely on a one-line description, so specific issues cannot be verified
Security-sensitive area: auth middleware refactor directly affects authentication/authorization enforcement
Session handling changes risk session fixation, improper invalidation on logout, or cookie flag regressions (HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite)
Token refresh logic changes risk refresh-token reuse/replay, missing rotation, or race conditions allowing expired tokens to be accepted
No mention of tests covering the refactored auth paths (valid/expired/tampered tokens, concurrent refresh, logout invalidation)
Potential for silent breaking changes to auth behavior affecting all protected routes
No mention of backward compatibility for existing active sessions/tokens during rollout
review_pr.comment: This is a security-sensitive refactor of auth middleware (session + token refresh), but no diff was provided, so I can only flag areas needing scrutiny rather than confirmed bugs. Before merge, please: (1) attach the actual diff, (2) confirm test coverage for expired/tampered/valid tokens, concurrent refresh, and session invalidation on logout, (3) verify token rotation prevents refresh-token replay, (4) check session cookie flags (HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite) are preserved, and (5) confirm existing active sessions won't be invalidated on deploy. Rating high risk until the diff and tests are reviewed.

suggest_reviewer.reasoning: This PR refactors the auth middleware—session handling and token refresh—which maps directly to the security/auth owner (@sec-lead), and the high risk level reinforces preferring the security owner. The riskiest findings are squarely in @sec-lead's domain: session-handling changes risking session fixation, broken logout invalidation, or cookie-flag regressions (HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite), and token-refresh changes risking refresh-token reuse/replay, missing rotation, or race conditions that let expired tokens through. With no diff or tests mentioned and potential silent breaking changes to every protected route plus rollout backward-compatibility for active sessions, this needs the security lead's scrutiny over any area owner like @be-lead.
